tips: Always normalize your data before feeding it into a neural network for faster convergence.
Use list comprehensions in Python for more concise and often faster code.
Clean data is more important than a complex model. Spend more time on EDA and preprocessing.
Regularization (L1/L2) helps prevent overfitting by penalizing large weights.
In Git, use descriptive commit messages to help your future self and teammates.
The zip() function is great for iterating over multiple lists in parallel.
Use virtual environments (venv or conda) to keep your project dependencies isolated.
Feature engineering can often provide more improvement than hyperparameter tuning.
Always keep a separate test set that your model never sees during training.
Use pandas.DataFrame.info() to quickly check for missing values and data types.
Random Forests are robust to outliers and don't require feature scaling.
Cross-validation (like K-Fold) provides a more reliable estimate of model performance.
Don't repeat yourself (DRY) - if you copy-paste code twice, it's time to write a function.
Vectorization with NumPy is significantly faster than using loops for numerical operations.
Precision vs. Recall: choose your metric based on the cost of false positives vs. false negatives.
Use tqdm to add progress bars to your long-running Python loops.
SQL: Indexing columns used in JOIN and WHERE clauses can drastically improve query speed.
Version control your data and models, not just your code (tools like DVC can help).
Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) often wins Kaggle competitions for tabular data.
Documentation is a love letter to your future self.
Use try...except blocks to handle exceptions gracefully and prevent your program from crashing.
Deep learning: Dropout is a powerful technique to prevent overfitting in neural networks.
In Python, use f-strings for more readable and efficient string formatting.
Always document your functions using docstrings (PEP 257) to explain their purpose and parameters.
Data Visualization: Choose the right chart type to effectively communicate your data's story.
Use itertools for efficient looping and iterator manipulation in Python.
When working with large datasets, consider using chunking in pandas to save memory.
Machine Learning: Early stopping is a simple way to prevent overfitting during training.
SQL: Use EXPLAIN to understand how your database executes a query and identify bottlenecks.
Keep your code modular by breaking it into smaller, reusable components.
Use the 'with' statement for resource management (like opening files) to ensure they are properly closed.
Python: 'enumerate()' is better than 'range(len())' when you need both the index and the value.
ML: SHAP or LIME can help explain the predictions of complex 'black box' models.
